items.title: Lock an account after a threshold of failed logins for a lockout window
items.description: After N consecutive failed login attempts the account must be locked for a defined lockout window, rejecting further attempts even with correct credentials until the window elapses or an admin intervenes. This blunts online password guessing and credential-stuffing without permanently locking legitimate users out.
items.applicability: The login path of any generated ERP that authenticates users with a username and password.
items.example: After 5 failures the account is locked for 15 minutes; a 6th attempt returns a generic 'invalid credentials or account locked' and writes an account-locked audit event.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store the failure counter and a lockout-until timestamp on the user, and check both before verifying the password. Test implication: drive N+1 failed attempts and assert the next attempt is blocked even with the correct password. Lockout mitigates online guessing only; it does not protect against offline cracking of a leaked hash, which depends on the hashing strength.

items.title: Enforce a password policy of minimum length plus character classes
items.description: Passwords must satisfy a policy of at least a minimum length and a required mix of character classes (for example upper, lower, digit and symbol) before being accepted at set or reset time. The policy is enforced server-side so weak passwords cannot be persisted regardless of the client.
items.applicability: User creation, self-service password change and admin password reset in a generated ERP.
items.example: A password of 'abc' is rejected with the policy text; 'Tr0ub4dor&3' satisfying length and four classes is accepted and hashed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: centralise the policy in one validator reused by every set-password path so it cannot be bypassed. Test implication: assert non-conforming passwords are rejected at create, change and admin-reset. A complexity policy raises the floor but does not detect reused or breached passwords; a breached-password check is a separate, optional extension.

items.title: Audit every security event
items.description: Each security-relevant event — successful login, logout, failed login, account locked and password reset — must be recorded to an audit log with actor, timestamp and outcome. This produces an investigable trail for incident response and satisfies the expectation that authentication activity is observable.
items.applicability: Every authentication and account-management action in a generated ERP.
items.example: A failed login writes an audit row {event: failed-login, username, utc, ip}; a successful one writes {event: login, userId, utc}; a lockout writes {event: account-locked, userId, utc}.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: write the audit row in the same flow as the event so an action cannot occur without its record. Test implication: perform each event type and assert exactly one matching audit row. Audit logs grow continuously and may themselves contain sensitive metadata, so retention and access to the log must be governed separately.

items.title: Reset the failure count and stamp last-login on success
items.description: A successful authentication must reset the consecutive-failure counter to zero and record a last-login timestamp. Resetting the counter prevents legitimate users from accruing toward a lockout across normal sessions, and the last-login stamp supports anomaly detection and account-activity review.
items.applicability: The success branch of the login flow in a generated ERP.
items.example: On a correct password the user's FailedLoginCount is set to 0 and LastLoginUtc to now, then the session is established and a login audit row is written.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: perform the reset and stamp atomically with establishing the session so a crash cannot leave a stale counter. Test implication: fail a few times, then succeed, and assert the counter is zero and last-login advanced. The last-login stamp records only the most recent success; full session history requires the audit log, not this single field.

items.title: Do not reveal whether a username exists
items.description: Login and password-reset responses must be uniform whether or not the username exists, returning the same generic message and similar timing. Distinct 'no such user' versus 'wrong password' responses let an attacker enumerate valid accounts, so the system must not disclose account existence.
items.applicability: Login and self-service reset endpoints of a generated ERP.
items.example: Both an unknown username and a known username with a wrong password return 'invalid credentials'; a reset request for any address responds 'if the account exists, instructions were sent'.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: branch internally but return one message, and verify a dummy hash for unknown users to keep timing similar. Test implication: assert the unknown-user and wrong-password responses are identical. Timing equalisation is best-effort; a sophisticated attacker may still infer signals from side channels, so this reduces rather than eliminates enumeration risk.

items.title: Require an anti-forgery token on all login and logout POST forms
items.description: Every state-changing authentication form — login and logout — must POST with an anti-forgery token, validated server-side. The action carries [ValidateAntiForgeryToken] and the view emits @Html.AntiForgeryToken(), so a cross-site request cannot silently log a user in or out.
items.applicability: All login and logout forms in a generated ERP using cookie-based sessions.
items.example: The login view renders @Html.AntiForgeryToken(); the LoginPost action is decorated [HttpPost][ValidateAntiForgeryToken]; a forged POST without the token is rejected with a 400.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply the attribute on every POST auth action, not just login, so logout CSRF is also covered. Test implication: POST without a valid token and assert it is rejected. Anti-forgery protects against CSRF only; it is not a defence against XSS, which can read or forge tokens and must be mitigated independently.

items.title: Harden the auth cookie: HttpOnly, SameSite, secure-behind-TLS, sliding expiry
items.description: The authentication cookie must be configured HttpOnly so script cannot read it, SameSite to limit cross-site sending, with a secure policy of SameAsRequest in local development that becomes Always behind TLS, and a sliding 30-minute expiry that renews on activity and lapses on idle.
items.applicability: Cookie-authentication configuration in the startup of a generated ERP.
items.example: Cookie options set HttpOnly=true, SameSite=Lax, SecurePolicy=SameAsRequest (Always in production), ExpireTimeSpan=30 min, SlidingExpiration=true; an idle session expires after 30 minutes.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive SecurePolicy from environment so production enforces Always while local HTTP still works. Test implication: assert the issued cookie carries HttpOnly and the configured SameSite, and that an idle session expires. Cookie hardening assumes TLS in production; over plain HTTP the Secure flag cannot protect the cookie in transit.

items.title: Admin password reset must enforce policy and rehash
items.description: When an administrator resets a user's password the new value must pass the same password policy and be stored as a fresh hash, never as plaintext or a reversible form. The reset is itself an audited security event and the old hash is discarded.
items.applicability: Administrative user-management screens of a generated ERP.
items.example: An admin resets a locked user's password; the value is validated against the policy, hashed with the per-user salt, persisted, and an audit row {event: password-reset, actor: admin, target: user} is written.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route admin reset through the same policy validator and hashing routine as self-service so behaviour cannot diverge. Test implication: assert an admin reset rejects a weak password and stores a hash, not plaintext. Admin reset lets a privileged user set a known password, so pairing it with the must-change-on-next-login flag is required to avoid a lingering admin-known credential.

items.title: Use a MustChangePassword flag to force rotation after reset
items.description: A MustChangePassword flag set on a user (typically after an admin reset or first provisioning) forces the next successful login to redirect to a change-password screen before granting access to the application. This ensures an administrator-known or temporary credential is replaced by a user-only secret.
items.applicability: Provisioning and admin-reset flows of a generated ERP.
items.example: After an admin reset, MustChangePassword=true; the user logs in, is redirected to change the password, and only after a policy-compliant change is the flag cleared and the app accessible.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the redirect in middleware or an early filter so no application route is reachable while the flag is set. Test implication: assert a flagged user is redirected to the change screen and cannot reach a protected page until they comply. The flag governs interactive logins; non-interactive or token paths, if added later, would need their own equivalent enforcement.

items.title: Test lockout, policy and audit behaviour
items.description: The generator should emit tests proving the hardening actually works: that N+1 failures lock the account, that sub-policy passwords are rejected at every set-password path, and that each security event writes its audit row. These tests turn the hardening rules into enforced, regression-protected behaviour.
items.applicability: CI coverage of the authentication subsystem in a generated ERP.
items.example: AuthHardeningTests drives repeated failed logins and asserts lockout, submits a weak password and asserts rejection, and performs a login/logout/failed-login and asserts three audit rows.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: exercise the real login pipeline (not just the validator in isolation) so wiring gaps are caught. Test implication: cover lockout, policy and audit as distinct cases. Passing these tests demonstrates the implemented controls work; it does not certify the system against a formal security standard, which requires independent review.

items.title: Hash passwords with a salted, slow algorithm and never store plaintext
items.description: Passwords must be stored only as salted hashes produced by a deliberately slow, adaptive algorithm, never as plaintext or a fast unsalted digest. Verification recomputes the hash to compare; the original is never recoverable. This limits the damage of a database disclosure.
items.applicability: Credential storage for every user account in a generated ERP.
items.example: On set-password the value is hashed with a per-user salt and a tunable work factor; on login the candidate is re-hashed and compared, with no plaintext or reversible secret ever persisted.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: use a vetted hashing library and store its parameters so the work factor can be raised over time. Test implication: assert the stored value is not the plaintext and that verification succeeds and fails correctly. A strong hash slows offline cracking but cannot rescue a trivially weak password, which is why the complexity policy and lockout are complementary, not optional.

items.title: Treat this as app-level auth with documented extension points for MFA and SSO
items.description: The hardening described is application-level username-and-password authentication. It deliberately does not yet include multi-factor authentication, single sign-on, OIDC or Windows authentication. These are documented as extension points so an integrator knows what is and is not provided rather than assuming enterprise SSO is present.
items.applicability: Setting expectations and integration planning for the authentication layer of a generated ERP.
items.example: Release notes state 'app-level auth only; MFA/SSO/OIDC/Windows-auth are not implemented' and point to the authentication service as the extension seam for adding an external identity provider.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: structure the auth service behind an interface so an external identity provider can be slotted in without rewriting callers. Test implication: assert the documented boundary is reflected in the shipped configuration (no SSO endpoints claimed). This honesty boundary means deployments requiring MFA or federated identity must add it explicitly; the generated baseline does not satisfy those requirements on its own.
